volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Two Bacchises (Section 4.4.685b - Section 4.4.737b)

sections.label: Section 4.4.685b
sections.original: Quid , ubi reddebas aurum , dixisti patri ?
sections.translation: What did you say to your father when you gave up the gold ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.686
sections.original: Mé id aurum accepisse extemplo ab hospite Archidemide .
sections.translation: That I had at once received this gold from his host , Archidemides .

sections.label: Section 4.4.686b
sections.translation: Eh ! by that speech you have this day consigned Chrysalus to the torture ; for , when he shall set eyes on me , the old gentleman will carry me off that instant to the executioner .

sections.label: Section 4.4.689
sections.translation: I ’ ve besought my father .

sections.label: Section 4.4.689b
sections.original: Nempe ergo hoc ut faceret quod loquor ?
sections.translation: I suppose , to do , in fact , the thing that I was speaking of ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.690
sections.original: Immo tibi ne noceat neu quid ob eam rem suscenseat ;
sections.translation: Nay , not to punish you , or to blame you at all on account of this business . And with some difficulty I ’ ve prevailed . Now , Chrysalus , this must be your care .

sections.label: Section 4.4.692
sections.translation: What do you wish should be my care ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.692b
sections.original: Vt ad senem etiam álteram facias viam .
sections.translation: That once again you should make a second inroad upon the old gentleman . Contrive , devise , invent whatever you please ; frame your plans [*] , so that this day you may cleverly deceive the old man unawares , and carry off the gold .

sections.label: Section 4.4.695
sections.translation: It scarcely seems possible to be done .

sections.label: Section 4.4.695b
sections.translation: Set about it , and you ’ ll easily effect it .

sections.label: Section 4.4.696
sections.original: Quam , malum , fácile , quem mendaci prendit manufesto modo ?
sections.translation: How the plague easily , for me , whom he has this moment caught out in a lie ? Should I entreat him not to believe me at all , he would not venture even to believe me in that .

sections.label: Section 4.4.698
sections.original: Immo si audias quae dicta dixit me adversum tibi .
sections.translation: Aye , and if you were to hear what things he said about you in my presence .

sections.label: Section 4.4.699
sections.translation: What did he say ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.699b
sections.original: Si tu illum solem sibi solem esse diceres ,
sections.translation: That if you were to say that this sun was the sun ,

sections.label: Section 4.4.700
sections.original: se illum lunam credere esse et noctem qui nunc est dies .
sections.translation: he would believe it was the moon , and that that is the night which is now the day .

sections.label: Section 4.4.701
sections.original: Emungam hercle hominem probe hodie , ne id nequiquam dixerit .
sections.translation: By my troth , I ’ ll bamboozle the old chap right well this very day , so that he shan ’ t have said that for nothing .

sections.label: Section 4.4.702
sections.translation: Now , what would you have us do ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.702b
sections.original: Énim nil nisi ut ametis impero .
sections.translation: Why , nothing , except that I beg you ’ ll still love on . As for the rest , ask of me as much gold as you please ; I ’ ll find it you . Of what use is it for me to have the name of Chrysalus [*] , unless I give proofs by fact ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.705
sections.original: sed nunc quantillum usust auri tibi , Mnesiloche ? dic mihi .
sections.translation: But now , tell me , Mnesilochus , how much gold is requisite for you .

sections.label: Section 4.4.706
sections.original: Militi nummis ducentis iam usus est pro Bacchide .
sections.translation: There ’ s occasion for two hundred pieces at once , to pay the Captain for Bacchis .

sections.label: Section 4.4.707
sections.translation: I ’ ll find it you .

sections.label: Section 4.4.707b
sections.translation: Then we have need of some for current expenses .

sections.label: Section 4.4.707c
sections.translation: Aye , aye , I wish us to do each thing deliberately ; when I ’ ve accomplished the one , then I ’ ll set about the other . First , for the two hundred pieces , I shall direct my engine of war against the old gentleman .

sections.label: Section 4.4.710
sections.original: ea ballista si pervortam turrim et propugnacula ,
sections.translation: If with that engine I batter down the tower and the outworks , straight at the gate that instant I ’ ll attack the old town and the new one ; if I take it , then carry to your friends the gold in baskets , just as your heart wishes .

sections.label: Section 4.4.713b
sections.translation: Our hearts are with you , Chrysalus .

sections.label: Section 4.4.714
sections.original: Nunc tu abi intro , Pistoclere , ad Bacchidem , atque ecfer cito
sections.translation: Now , do you go in-doors to Bacchis , Pistoclerus , and quickly bring out —

sections.label: Section 4.4.715
sections.translation: What ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.715b
sections.translation: A pen , some wax [*] , tablets , and some cord .

sections.label: Section 4.4.715c
sections.translation: I ’ ll have them here this instant .

sections.label: Section 4.4.716
sections.original: Quid nunc es facturus ? id mihi dice .
sections.translation: What now are you about to do ? Do tell me that .

sections.label: Section 4.4.716b
sections.translation: Let a breakfast be prepared ; there will be you two , and your mistress will make a third with you .

sections.label: Section 4.4.718
sections.translation: Just as you say .

sections.label: Section 4.4.718b
sections.translation: Pistoclerus has no mistress ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.718c
sections.translation: O yes , she ’ s here ; he ’ s in love with the one sister , I with the other , both of the name of Bacchis .

sections.label: Section 4.4.720
sections.translation: What were you going to say ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.720b
sections.translation: This ; how we are to manage [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.4.720c
sections.translation: Where are your couches [*] laid out ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.721b
sections.translation: Why o you ask that ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.721c
sections.translation: So the matter stands ; I wish to be informed . You know not what I am about to do , nor what a great exploit I shall attempt .

sections.label: Section 4.4.723
sections.original: Cedo manum ac subsequere propius me ad fores . intro inspice .
sections.translation: Give me your hand , and follow me close , to the door . Peep in .

sections.label: Section 4.4.724
sections.original: Euax , nimis bellus átque ut esse maxume optabam locus .
sections.translation: Hurra ! ’ Tis a very pretty place , this , and exactly as I could wish it to be .

sections.label: Section 4.4.726
sections.original: Quae imperavisti . imperatum bene bonis factum ilicost .
sections.translation: The things that you ordered — what ’ s ordered for a good purpose is forthwith done by the obedient .

sections.label: Section 4.4.727
sections.translation: What have you brought ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.727b
sections.translation: Everything that you bade me bring .

sections.label: Section 4.4.728
sections.original: Cape stilum propere et tabellas tu has tibi .
sections.translation: Do you take the pen at once , and these tablets .

sections.label: Section 4.4.728b
sections.translation: What then ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.729
sections.original: Quod iubebo scribito istic . nam propterea te volo
sections.translation: Write there what I shall bid you ; for I wish you to write for this reason ,

sections.label: Section 4.4.730
sections.original: scribere , ut pater cognoscat litteras quando legat .
sections.translation: that your father may know the hand when he reads it .

sections.label: Section 4.4.731
sections.translation: Write now .

sections.label: Section 4.4.731b
sections.translation: What shall I write ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.731c
sections.translation: Health to your father , in your own language .

sections.label: Section 4.4.732
sections.original: Quid si potius morbum mortem scribat ? id erit rectius .
sections.translation: Suppose he were to write disease and death , in preference , that would be much better .

sections.label: Section 4.4.733
sections.translation: Don ’ t you interrupt us .

sections.label: Section 4.4.733b
sections.translation: What you have ordered is now written on the wax .

sections.label: Section 4.4.733c
sections.translation: Tell me in what terms .

sections.label: Section 4.4.734
sections.translation: Mnesilochus sends health to his father .

sections.label: Section 4.4.734b
sections.translation: Write this , too , quickly :

sections.label: Section 4.4.735
sections.original: “ Chrysalus mihi usque quaque loquitur nec recte , pater ,
sections.translation: My father , Chrysalus is always and everywhere talking at me , and in no measured terms , because I gave you up the gold , and because I did not cheat you of it .

sections.label: Section 4.4.737
sections.translation: Stop till he has written it .

sections.label: Section 4.4.737b
sections.translation: It befits the hand of a lover to be active .
